title: Repair-neighbour reconstruction candidate
notes: Twenty-first-round mutation generated after noisy-channel overacceptance exposed a gap in the processing/naturalness module. It tests whether distance to a plausible intended repair neighbour must be represented separately from semantic-pragmatic recoverability. It is an unscored candidate, not a scoped module yet.

edges.rationale: Greater distance from a plausible intended repair neighbour tends to lower recovery of the intended form or move.
Greater distance from a nearby intended form tends to lower felt naturalness in first-pass or noisy-channel judgments.
Greater distance makes a specific repair neighbour less available, lowering targeted reformulation pressure even when rejection remains possible.
Recovering an intended form or move can increase felt naturalness even when the presented string is formally defective.
Processing burden can lower felt naturalness independently of repair-neighbour availability.
Felt naturalness can appear as reported acceptability in fast or comprehension-oriented tasks.
Task format affects whether respondents report reconstructed meaning, literal form status, or ordinary acceptability.
A proofreading, literal-analysis, or fast-comprehension frame changes whether overacceptance becomes a grammar attribution.
Community licensing remains evidence for attribution, but noisy-channel reconstruction is not licensing.
Reported acceptability provides contextual evidence for attribution but can be inflated by repair-neighbour reconstruction.
Targeted repair pressure can provide evidence about the literal presented string without equaling grammaticality attribution.
